Urgent Issue: API Integration with Mercury Language and Data Discrepancies,"Hello Aetheros Support Team,

We are facing a critical issue with our API integration that's impacting our production environment. We're using the Mercury language for our API development, and we're encountering significant data discrepancies.

Specifically, the data retrieved via our Mercury API calls is inconsistent with what's reflected in our primary database. This is causing major issues with data integrity and application functionality.
We need immediate assistance in resolving this issue. Please provide urgent support to:
1. Identify the root cause of the data discrepancies between our API and database.
2. Provide a solution to rectify the existing data inconsistencies.
3. Offer guidance on preventing such issues in the future.
We are available for a call to discuss this further at your earliest convenience.

Watch the winnerвҖҷs Tiny Desk concert,"Little Moon performs вҖңWonder Eye,вҖқ plus new songs View this email online вҖңI love being here,вҖқ Emma Hardyman says in Little MoonвҖҷs Tiny Desk concert. вҖңI say that with the utmost relief!вҖқ After watching nearly 6,000 Contest entries, we at NPR were so thrilled to finally welcome Emma and the band (including a big, beautiful harp) to the Tiny Desk last week. Little Moon put on an exquisite performance packed with emotion and creativity, playing its Contest-winning song, вҖңWonder Eye,вҖқ plus two unreleased tracks (written for a new album).Bob Boilen shared a bit about the day here, noting that he and Bobby Carter вҖңwere in awe hearing singer Emma HardymanвҖҷs four-octave voice warming up behind the Desk.вҖқ Bob explained how challenging it can be for artists to get the balance and sound levels correct at the Desk without monitors or vocal amplification. But Little MoonвҖҷs band members вҖңall pretty much nailed it,вҖқ he said.Watch Little Moon's Tiny DeskIt was so special to witness the bandвҖҷs incredible musicianship, quirky, sweet banter and playful nature behind the Desk. Emma shared her inspiration behind the second song in the set, вҖңWe Fall In Our Sleep,вҖқ which is about вҖңcrazy dreams.вҖқ And she shared that the last song, вҖңKind, Kind Home,вҖқ is about вҖңfeeling like you donвҖҷt have a home вҖҰ and discovering that maybe if you donвҖҷt have a home or feel a little out of place, you can be a kind, kind home.вҖқ WeвҖҷre so glad that Little Moon has found a place in our Tiny home. And we hope the Contest has helped all of you in this community find a place of belonging, too.The 2023 Tiny Desk Contest is presented by Capital One and supported by GuayakГӯ Yerba Mate. Newsletter continues after sponsor message We were so excited to meet our winner in real life and hear them perform вҖ” and now it's your turn. You can still get tickets to see Little Moon, plus a handful of other standout artists, at the Tiny Desk Contest On The Road tour this summer at NPRPresents.org.For now, please join us in congratulating Little Moon! We know this Tiny Desk concert is just the beginning for this band.From the Desk, NPRвҖҷs Tiny Desk Contest team Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). What do you think of today's email? We'd love to hear your thoughts, questions and feedback: tinydeskcontest@npr.orgInterested in connecting with music-lovers and music-makers across the Contest community? Join our Tiny Desk Contest Community Facebook Group!Need a new playlist? Follow NPR Music on Spotify and Apple Music!Looking for more great content? Check out all of our newsletter offerings вҖ” including Music, Books, Pop Culture and more! You received this message because you're subscribed to Tiny Desk Contest emails. This email was sent by National Public Radio, Inc., 1111 North Capitol Street NE, Washington, DC 20002Unsubscribe | Privacy Policy",1

URGENT: Mercury Language Compiler Errors - Production Down!,"Our production environment is currently down due to critical errors we are encountering with the Mercury Language compiler.

We are getting consistent ""Type mismatch on line 42"" errors, but the code seems perfectly valid. We haven't made any recent changes to the codebase, and this issue seemingly appeared out of nowhere.
We are losing thousands of dollars every minute our system is down. Please advise ASAP on how to resolve this issue.",0

URGENT: Mercury Language Integration with IAM Failing in Production!,"We are experiencing a critical issue in our production environment where our Mercury-based API is unable to authenticate with the Aetheros IAM service.
This is causing a complete outage of our application. We have verified that our IAM credentials are correct and there's no network connectivity issue.
We suspect there might be a bug in the latest Mercury language runtime or its integration with the IAM service.
Please investigate this issue with the utmost urgency and provide a solution as soon as possible.",0

IвҖҷm Amber Shortt, the StarвҖҷs housing editor. HereвҖҷs the latest on broken condo elevators, young investors turning to real estate and when to list your house. GOOD WEEK FOR... Housing advocates. A new report says TorontoвҖҷs post-pandemic oversupply of office space could last for decades, bolstering arguments to turn the space toward residential buildings. Recent homebuyers. Long maligned, the mortgage stress test is getting credit for blunting pain among new owners as rates jumped 4.25 percentage points over the space of 15 months. Mortgage brokers. Many fixed-rate fans are opting for a three-year term over the slightly lower five-year fixed rate in anticipation of possible lower rates next year. Where should you eat next? If youвҖҷre wondering where to eat as the city heats up, you need Food Crawl, the StarвҖҷs free food newsletter. Whether itвҖҷs a strip mall standout or a downtown gem, Food Crawl will make you an insider on the best eats in Toronto and beyond. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... The city. Toronto is suing developers for allegedly failing to repay the $200,000 cost of protecting a shelter from neighbouring condo construction вҖ” claims the developers deny. This landlord. A man was sentenced to 20 years in prison for allowing a tenant to set up an opioid drug lab in the basement of his Pickering rental home, while a family unwittingly rented upstairs. Residents on the 40th floor. The near monopoly of elevator installation and repair in Toronto means plenty of condo elevators are out of service. HereвҖҷs what the regulatorвҖҷs doing. TRENDING It used to be that when young people graduated school, they were consumed with finding that first job. Now, increasingly, theyвҖҷre looking to nab that first investment property, too. A recent survey for Royal LePage found more than a quarter of Canadians who had yet to jump into the investment market planned to do so within the next five years, with about half of them between the ages of 18 and 34. And theyвҖҷre willing to couch surf to do it. The survey found 33 per cent of younger investors bought their first income property before a home to live in themselves, either while renting or living with family. TheyвҖҷre also the most likely of any cohort to buy more than one income property, often pairing with a financing partner, be it family, friend or other, to afford it. And who can blame them? Considering, for example, the average cancer researcher makes roughly $57,000 a year вҖ” just over half the required $100,000 income to comfortably lease a one-bedroom apartment in Toronto, and barely enough to meet the $55,000 salary to uncomfortably rent one вҖ” it wouldnвҖҷt be surprising if todayвҖҷs youth see real estate as more lucrative than their day job. It's a notion that was floated by Ben Rabidoux, president of market research firm North Cove Advisors, back in 2021 at the height of the pandemic market when speculation purchases were running rampant. вҖңI donвҖҷt know how many people who would have ended up in the STEM field are instead flipping houses. ItвҖҷs so lucrative right now you canвҖҷt blame them, but I donвҖҷt know if, from a longer-term perspective, that enhances productivity in our country,вҖқ he told the Star at the time. It seems Gen Z and millennial investors are just taking a page out of the boomer playbook. Those aged 55 and older were vastly overrepresented among real estate investors in Ontario, making up 57 per cent despite being just 32 per cent of the population, a recent Statistics Canada report showed. For them, the investment has paid off вҖ” whether that will repeat for todayвҖҷs young investors remains to be seen. вҖңIf you go back over 65 years, the real estate market continues to outperform many of the other indices вҖ” the TSX or the NASDAQ,вҖқ Mike Heddle of Royal LePage State Realty told the StarвҖҷs Tess Kalinowski. But another report, this time from CIBC and Urbanation, warns that many investors who bought pre-construction condos just a few years ago are now underwater, as rising interest rates and costs have put half of condo investments in the red. Still, after watching everyone from firefighters to chefs to nurses not be able to afford to both work and live in Toronto, it seems the cityвҖҷs blistering housing market may have turned the old adage of вҖңdonвҖҷt quit your day jobвҖқ for your side project on its head. Now, if you want a day job, be it teaching high school or curing cancer, the advice might be вҖңdonвҖҷt quit your realtor.вҖқ LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND For those who want to get away from it all while still having it all close by, this $700,000 Wasaga cottage is a five-minute drive from a Walmart. Check it out. Residents along Eglinton Avenue face seemingly endless construction вҖ” and they live on the second-worst road in Ontario. These other roads made the list. CanвҖҷt afford a house in Toronto? Maybe you can buy a castle in Monaco вҖ” or any of these other glamorous properties cheaper than local real estate. HereвҖҷs what the creators of the new book Denison Avenue have to say about the history and current state of TorontoвҖҷs Chinatown and Kensington Market. Japanese researchers made a house made partially of shredded disposable diapers. HereвҖҷs why this breakthrough is the s---. [Science News] Function doesnвҖҷt trump form in this 10,000-square-foot King home, where all you see is вҖңthe really pretty things.вҖқ Look inside. SHOULD I LIST MY HOME NOW OR WAIT TILL THE FALL? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS With prices rising but the traditionally slow days of summer real estate on their way, the Star talked to One Group broker Nasma Ali about whether itвҖҷs best to list a home now or wait until the fall market. HereвҖҷs what she said: Whether you decide to sell now or wait until fall depends on your circumstances, and thereвҖҷs advantages to both. If you have already purchased a home to move into, or a tenant has moved out and you'd like to avoid vacancy months, then selling now is best. In the summer, families with children are also trying to buy and settle down before the start of the new school year. However, summer activity becomes exceptionally slow in August вҖ” specifically the last half of August when everyone is scrambling to enjoy the last days of sunshine and vacation. If you get to August and you have time to wait, then it may be worth waiting until after Labour Day. My advice would be to be patient and list the second week of September, as there tends to be a flood of listings that first week. Waiting lets you observe any new sale benchmarks or records set and price accordingly. The fall market offers you a small window of prime activity, until around mid October. After that, activity becomes more steady. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week. A New Theory on Mapping the New World
In 1507, a group of scholars working in France produced an extraordinary map
of the world, the first to put the still-recent discoveries of Columbus and
others into a new continent separate from Asia, and to call that continent
America."" With the Waldseemuller map, the New World was born.
But there was something else. What would later come to be called South
America and Central America were surprisingly well-shaped, not only on the
east coast, where explorers had already sailed, but also on the west coast
-- which no European was known to have seen.
The ice cream cone bulge that sticks out into the Pacific at the junction of
modern-day Chile and Peru is readily visible and in almost exactly the right
geographical spot -- not only in the main map, but also in an inset printed
The shape of South America in the main map appears distorted because of the
It is an improbable coincidence, if it was a coincidence, for the map -- 12
large printed pages to be arrayed in one 36-square-foot wall display -- was
published six years before Vasco Balboa's 1513 trip across the Isthmus of
Panama and 12 years before Ferdinand Magellan's 1519-22 trip around the
